title: Strain-free, symmetrical, InGaAs quantum dots as single photon emitters in the telecomC-band
authors: Rabbia Tahir, Paweł Wyborski, Artur Tuktamyshev, Stefano Vichi, Richard Nötzel, Battulga Munkhbat, Stefano Sanguinetti
abstract: Non-classical photon sources made of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) emitting in the telecommunication C-band are crucial components for low-loss, long-distance photonic quantum communication networks. Here we designed and fabricated strain--free In$_{0.7}$Ga$_{0.3}$As/In$_{0.7}$Al$_{0.3}$As QDs grown on GaAs(111)A substrates working as single-photon emitters in the 1550 nm window. The QDs were grown via local droplet etching method in a molecular beam epitaxy environment, employing a thin In$_{0.7}$Al$_{0.3}$As metamorphic buffer layer with the same lattice constant of the QD material, thus allowing for a completely strain--free self-assembly of the QDs. The QDs exhibit a C$_{3v}$ symmetry with a ground state emission in the 1400--1600 nm range. The exciton lifetimes of $\approx$ 1.3--1.9 ns and linewidths as low as $\approx$ 300 $μ$eV show the good quality of the fabricated QDs. Second-order autocorrelation measurements under pulsed excitation confirmed the single-photon purity of the emitters, yielding a $g^{(2)}(0)$ value of $0.141 \pm 0.027$
summary: The paper demonstrates a method to grow strain-free, symmetric InGaAs quantum dots that emit in the telecommunications C-band. By using local droplet etching on GaAs(111)A substrates, the researchers achieved high-quality single-photon emission with low asymmetry. This is a significant step toward building long-distance quantum communication networks using existing fiber technology.
main_problem: The need for high-quality, strain-free single-photon emitters operating in the telecommunication C-band (1550 nm) for low-loss, long-distance quantum communication networks.
main_result: Successful fabrication of strain-free, symmetric InGaAs/InAlAs quantum dots on GaAs(111)A substrates that emit in the 1400-1600 nm range with high single-photon purity (g2(0) = 0.141).
method: The researchers used Local Droplet Etching (LDE) via Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) to create nanoholes, followed by filling them with InGaAs, and validated the results using 8-band k.p simulations and optical spectroscopy.
model_or_system: Strain-free, C3v-symmetric In0.7Ga0.3As/In0.7Al0.3As quantum dots grown on a thin metamorphic buffer layer on vicinal GaAs(111)A substrates.
key_observables: Emission wavelength (1400-1600 nm), exciton lifetimes (1.3-1.9 ns), linewidths (approx. 300 ueV), second-order autocorrelation g2(0), and degree of linear polarization (DOLP).
important_parameters: Target wavelength of 1550 nm, substrate temperature of 450-500 C, and QD height of approximately 6 nm.
main_assumptions: The use of the envelope function approximation (EFA) within an eight-band k.p framework to model electronic properties.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the QD fabrication schematic; Figure 2 displays AFM morphology of nanoholes and simulated emission properties; Figure 3 shows micro-photoluminescence spectra and photon statistics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of C-band emission, describes the LDE growth process and metamorphic buffer layer, presents morphological characterization via AFM, details the k.p simulations, and concludes with optical characterization of the emitters.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a scalable material platform for generating high-purity single photons at the telecom C-band, which is essential for integrating quantum nodes into existing fiber-optic infrastructure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic diagram, summarizing steps for the InGaAs/InAlAs QDs growth by LDE on GaAs(111)A substrate with InAlAs MMBL such as (a) formation of droplets after depositing In, (b) local surface etching in As4 background atmosphere by the indium droplets and creation of nanoholes, (c) filling the nanoholes with InGaAs to fabricate required QDs, (d) growth of a barrier capping layer.
Figure 2: (a) 2.5×2.5 µm2 AFM topography image of sample A. The inset reports the magnified image of 6.8 nm deep and 54.3 nm wide individual nanohole. (b) 2.5×2.5 µm2

Figure 3: (a) µ-PL spectra of several QDs with sharp emission peaks centered at around 1380–1600 nm. (b) µ-PL spectra of marked QD in (a) (within C-band range), including fitted line broadening value. (c) Power-dependent QD PL intensity fitted by a power law. (d) Emission intensity plot as a function of polarization, including DOLP fitting. (e) Time- resolved PL histogram of QD emission, with the solid lines depicting a monoexponential fit to the experimental data.
Figure 4: (a) Autocorrelation coincidence plot. (b) Second order correlation function g2(0) for CX emission, the solid purple line represents the direct fit for the result.
Figure 4(a, b) depicts the autocorrelation measurements of one of the brightest grown
